one_line_takeaway: delta-Unlearning wraps a frozen black-box LLM with a small trainable/frozen offset-model pair so that a learned logit offset performs the unlearning, matching direct fine-tuning's forgetting while better preserving general utility (MU up ~15+ points for Gradient Ascent and KL) and storing no sensitive data at inference.
key_finding: At matched Forget-Set ROUGE, delta-Unlearning attains forget quality on par with direct fine-tuning while preserving equal or better out-of-forget-scope utility, and it works across all four unlearning algorithms. The largest gains are in retained Model Utility: with Gradient Ascent, delta-Unlearning reaches MU 48.6 vs 32.7 for direct fine-tuning, and with KL Minimization 50.4 vs 33.5 (each >15 points), approaching the Before-Unlearning/Retraining level (~62-63). It also generally yields higher Truth Ratio and Forget Quality than direct fine-tuning on the forget set (e.g., Gradient Ascent FQ 4.0e-6 vs 2.6e-7). The offset strength alpha behaves as a controllable inference-time knob: too low leaves the target model unchanged, alpha=1 gives fluent answers with the sensitive fact removed, and alpha=5 drops Forget ROUGE below 10 (below Retraining's 38.9) but renders outputs gibberish/unusable. Stronger (larger) offset models give better retain performance (Qwen target: 7B+3B offset MU 52.2 vs 7B+0.5B 48.8, vs 7B direct fine-tuning 37.8, Retraining 58.8).
core_contribution: The paper proposes delta-Unlearning, an offset-based framework for unlearning target data from a BLACK-BOX large language model without touching its weights. Instead of fine-tuning the large model M, the method keeps M frozen and introduces a pair of smaller white-box 'offset' models Mo (frozen) and M'o (trainable), both initialized from the same checkpoint so their logit difference is zero at the start. At decoding time the ensemble logits are l_e = l_M + alpha*(l_M'o - l_Mo); only M'o is fine-tuned with any standard unlearning objective computed on the ENSEMBLE prediction, so it learns the logit offset that steers M away from generating the forgotten information. This is framed as a product-of-experts (P_e proportional to P_M * (P_M'o / P_Mo)^alpha). The distinct claimed contributions are: (1) enabling weight-free unlearning for arbitrary black-box LLMs that only expose logits; (2) matching direct fine-tuning on forget quality while equalling or exceeding it on out-of-forget-scope utility; (3) being a plug-and-play wrapper compatible with multiple existing unlearning algorithms (Gradient Ascent, Gradient Difference, KL Minimization, Data Relabeling). NOTE ON ATTRIBUTION: the logit-ensembling / proxy-tuning idea (Liu et al. 2024a Proxy-Tuning; contrastive decoding; DExperts; Mitchell et al. 2024 emulated fine-tuning) is prior/related work the paper builds on; the TOFU benchmark and the four unlearning algorithms are also from prior work. The paper's own novelty is applying the offset/product-of-experts idea specifically to the black-box UNLEARNING setting and the accompanying privacy argument (no sensitive data retained at inference).
threat_model: The paper is motivated by privacy/legal risk from LLM memorization of copyrighted, biased, or private training data (e.g., reproducing copyrighted text, revealing private information), and by data-protection requirements such as the 'right to be forgotten'. Two concrete adversarial/deployment concerns drive the design: (1) many prior unlearning methods require access to internal weights, which is impossible for deployed black-box/API LLMs; (2) the only prior black-box option, in-context unlearning, must keep an explicit growing list of the sensitive items in the prompt at inference time, which itself violates privacy and is exposed to prompt-leaking attacks (Perez & Ribeiro 2022). delta-Unlearning's stated security merit is that after training it retains NO explicit sensitive data for inference and needs no access to the target model's weights. The paper does not, however, run any privacy-attack / membership-inference / extraction-attack evaluation; 'forgetting' is measured behaviorally via the TOFU metrics rather than via an adversary attempting to recover the forgotten data.
limitations: The reported novelty rests on an existing mechanism (proxy-tuning / logit offset), so contribution is the application to black-box unlearning rather than a new algorithm. Despite the privacy framing, there is NO adversarial privacy evaluation (no membership-inference or extraction-attack test that the forgotten data is truly unrecoverable); forgetting is only measured behaviorally on TOFU's synthetic fictitious-author data, which is a controlled/toy setting that may not reflect real copyrighted or private corpora. The 'black-box' claim still requires access to the target model's full output LOGITS and an offset model that shares the target's tokenizer/vocabulary, so it does not apply to truly closed APIs that return only text or top-k tokens. Inference cost roughly triples (three forward passes: M, Mo, M'o). Data Relabeling behaves anomalously (very high Probability, very low Forget Quality) because it does not explicitly minimize the original answer likelihood. Experiments use only Llama2-13b/7b and one Qwen family scaling study; no larger target models, and one PDF sentence is garbled ('while Llama2 models All models are trained').

datasets_used: TOFU unlearning benchmark (fictitious authors): Forget Set, Retain Set, Real Author, World Fact
quantitative_results: Before Unlearning: Forget-Set ROUGE-L 95.6, Probability 98.3, Truth Ratio 49.5, Forget Quality 1.3e-13, Model Utility 62.1
Retraining (upper bound): Forget ROUGE 38.9, Prob 15.2, TR 65.6, FQ 1.0, MU 62.9
Gradient Ascent - Direct FT vs delta-Unlearning: Forget ROUGE 38.8 vs 38.6; MU 32.7 vs 48.6 (delta better by ~16 pts); FQ 2.6e-7 vs 4.0e-6
KL Minimization - Direct FT vs delta-Unlearning: MU 33.5 vs 50.4 (delta better by ~17 pts); TR 53.4 vs 57.5
Gradient Difference - Direct FT vs delta-Unlearning: MU 51.4 vs 50.5 (roughly matched)
Data Relabeling - Direct FT vs delta-Unlearning: MU 56.1 vs 58.6; both retain high Forget-Set Probability (92.5 vs 91.5) and low Forget Quality (6.6e-12 vs 3.0e-13)
Offset-strength alpha ablation: alpha=1 removes the sensitive fact fluently; alpha=5 drops Forget ROUGE below 10 (vs Retraining 38.9) but outputs become gibberish/unusable
Offset-model size (Qwen2.5-7b target): Retraining MU 58.8; 7B Direct FT MU 37.8; 7B+3B offset MU 52.2; 7B+1.5B MU 49.1; 7B+0.5B MU 48.8 (larger offset -> better retain)
General benchmarks after unlearning stay near Before-Unlearning (ARC 44.0, HellaSwag 58.1, WinoGrande 67.9, OpenBookQA 36.2); e.g. Gradient Ascent delta-Unlearning ARC 42.2 vs direct FT 39.9
baselines_compared: Retraining (fine-tune with forget set excluded; upper bound)
Direct fine-tuning of the large model with each unlearning algorithm
(Discussed as prior black-box baseline) In-context unlearning (Pawelczyk et al. 2023)
